text.title: TBD Core Rules: Branch Lifetime, PR Size, CI Speed, Feature Flags
text.summary: Hard SLAs for branch lifetime and PR size, CI speed requirements, feature flag discipline, and TBD vs GitFlow comparison.

rule.statement: Branch lifetime must be at most 48 hours; PR diff must be at most 200 lines (excluding lockfiles). Enforce both in CI, not just in review.
rule.rationale: Branch lifetime SLA prevents merge debt accumulation. Size SLA keeps PRs reviewable in minutes. Enforcement in CI removes the social friction of asking reviewers to reject large PRs.

rule.statement: CI pipeline must complete in under 10 minutes; treat a CI time increase past 10 minutes as a P1 incident.
rule.rationale: TBD requires fast feedback. CI >10 min means developers queue work or commit without waiting — defeating the "trunk always green" guarantee.

rule.statement: Wrap every user-visible change behind a feature flag, even for solo development; pair each flag addition with a scheduled flag-retirement task.
rule.rationale: Kill-switching beats reverting a deploy. Flag addition without a retirement task creates flag debt; LLMs add flags and never remove them without an explicit prompt.

section.title: TBD vs GitFlow
section.p: TBD: 1 branch (trunk), lifetime hours to 2 days, conflicts rare and small, deployment continuous, complexity simple. Best for web apps / SaaS. GitFlow: 5+ branches (main, develop, feature/, release/, hotfix/), lifetime days to weeks, conflicts common and large, deployment scheduled, complexity high. Best for mobile / packaged software.

rule.statement: Never push directly to main; never use --no-verify; never --force-push on shared branches. Configure server-side protection to enforce these.
rule.rationale: Direct pushes to main bypass CI. --no-verify bypasses pre-commit hooks. Force-push on shared branches drops teammates' commits.
